coredata.dc:title: An ultra-sensitive detection of Melamine in milk using Rare-earth doped Graphene Quantum Dots- Synthesis and Optical Spectroscopic approach
coredata.dc:creator: Anjila P.K, Fathima
Tharani, G.R.
Sundaramoorthy, Anand
Kumar Shanmugam, Venkat
Subramani, Karthikeyan
Chinnathambi, Shanmugavel
Pandian, Ganesh N.
Raghavan, Vimala
Grace, Andrews Nirmala
Ganesan, Singaravelu
Rajendiran, Mangaiyarkarasi
coredata.dc:description: This work focuses on a rapid and sensitive spectroscopic approach for detecting melamine in food products based on rare-earth Terbium-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (Tb-GQDs) synthesized using microwave and simple chemical conjugation methods. Citric acid and diethylene triamine (DETA) are the precursors of synthesizing GQDs. Terbium, a rare earth element with exceptional fluorescence properties, is doped onto the surface of the GQD to obtain dual photoemission with a broad spectral window and to provide excellent photo-stable characteristics. The as-synthesized Tb-GQDs were characterized using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The synthesized Tb-GQD were spherical with a size of around 6 nm, showing approximately a quantum yield of 52 %, concerning the Standard-Quinine sulfate quantum yield of 54 %. Further, Tb-doped GQDs act as a fluorescent probe, were used to detect and quantify the concentration of melamine (0––5 µM) used as an adulterant in milk and milk products and obtained a maximum detection limit of 0.31 µM. This work concludes that the reported dual emitting Tb-GQDs provide a sensitive and effective platform for detecting melamine in real-time samples, especially in milk, and show promising potential in food industries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Terbium doped Graphene Quantum Dots
Melamine
Spectroscopic techniques
Fluorescence steady state methods
1–5 ÂµM detection
Melamine detection in milk samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of melamine in different preserved milk samples
Detection of melamine in real-time sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) for providing a Start-up Research Grant to Dr. Mangaiyarkarasi Rajendiran, Centre for Nanotechnology Research, VIT- Vellore with project file number SRG/ 2021/ 002073. The author(s) acknowledge VIT-Vellore for providing VIT SEED GRANT for this research work. Dr. Shanmugavel Chinnathambi was supported by a Grant-in-Aid for Early- Career Scientists fund (KAKENHI No. 22K15249) by Japan Society for the Promotion of Science.
Dr. Shanmugavel Chinnathambi was supported by a Grant-in-Aid for Early- Career Scientists fund (KAKENHI No. 22K15249) by Japan Society for the Promotion of Science.

The authors are thankful to the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) for providing a Start-up Research Grant to Dr. Mangaiyarkarasi Rajendiran, Centre for Nanotechnology Research, VIT- Vellore with project file number SRG/ 2021/ 002073.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (i) shows X-ray diffraction spectra of GQD and Tb-GQDs, (ii) HR-TEM images of GQD and Tb- GQD respectively (a, d). (b, e) shows diffraction pattern and (c, f) shows the lattice fringes of the QDs & (iii) FTIR spectra of GQD and Tb-GQDs.

(i) show the UV–Visible absorption, insets shows the photographs of GQDs and Tb-GQDs under UV light (ii) Raman Spectra of GQDs, Tb-GQDs, and Melamine respectively, (iii, iv) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs, and Tb-GQDs excited at different wavelengths.
Fluorescence lifetime profile of GQD, Tb-GQD, and Tb-GQD with melamine.

(i) UV–Visible absorption spectra of Tb- GQDs without and with the different concentration Melamine varies 0, 0.5 μM,1.25 μM,2 μM,2.5 μM,3 μM, 3.50 μM, 4 μM, 5 μM. The absorption spectrum of Tb- GQDs alone is shown in inlet. (ii) Fluorescence emission Spectra of Tb- GQDs in the absence and presence of different concentrations of Melamine ranging from 0 to 5 μM (0.83 μM,1.42 μM.2.2 μM,2.7 μM,3.07 μM,3.33 μM,3.7 μM,4 μM,4.2 μM,4.3 μM,4.4 μM). (iii) the detection limit of melamine (iv) Modified Stern Vomer plot of Melamine (0–5 μM) using Tb-GQD and the stern-Volmer plot has shown in inlet.
Schematic diagram illustrating the synthetic process of GQDs.
Fluorescence decay fitting parameters for GQD and Tb-GQD in absence and presence of melamine.
Determination of melamine in milk samples using Tb-GQDs.

head.ce:title: An ultra-sensitive detection of Melamine in milk using Rare-earth doped Graphene Quantum Dots- Synthesis and Optical Spectroscopic approach

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Fathima Anjila and Tharani G.R are equally contributed to this work as first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work focuses on a rapid and sensitive spectroscopic approach for detecting melamine in food products based on rare-earth Terbium-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (Tb-GQDs) synthesized using microwave and simple chemical conjugation methods. Citric acid and diethylene triamine (DETA) are the precursors of synthesizing GQDs. Terbium, a rare earth element with exceptional fluorescence properties, is doped onto the surface of the GQD to obtain dual photoemission with a broad spectral window and to provide excellent photo-stable characteristics. The as-synthesized Tb-GQDs were characterized using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The synthesized Tb-GQD were spherical with a size of around 6 nm, showing approximately a quantum yield of 52 %, concerning the Standard-Quinine sulfate quantum yield of 54 %. Further, Tb-doped GQDs act as a fluorescent probe, were used to detect and quantify the concentration of melamine (0––5 µM) used as an adulterant in milk and milk products and obtained a maximum detection limit of 0.31 µM. This work concludes that the reported dual emitting Tb-GQDs provide a sensitive and effective platform for detecting melamine in real-time samples, especially in milk, and show promising potential in food industries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Food is one of the most fundamental necessities for all living beings. As most food Industries practice food adulteration, there is a massive challenge in getting chemical–free food products worldwide. Food adulteration adds hazardous, unneeded, and unnecessary ingredients as preservatives in food items in the food industry. Another major problem of food adulteration is that it decreases the quality of food items and significantly impacts our health without us knowing [1,2]. Of many, one commonly seen adulteration product is adding melamine to dairy products. Melamine (C3H6N6) is an organic molecule rich in nitrogen (67 % per mass unit). It is widely utilized as an industrial chemical for manufacturing plastics, production of fertilizers, kitchenware, adhesives, laminates, coatings, moulding compounds, and flame retardants, respectively [3]. Due to the high nitrogen levels, melamine is illegally added to dairy products and animal feed to raise the estimated protein content [4]. According to The United States FDA (Food and Drug Administration) and FSSAI (Food Safety & Standard Authority of India), the maximum permissible limit of melamine added should be 1 mg/ kg of powdered infant formula, whereas 0.15 mg/ kg in liquid infant formula and 2.5 mg/kg in other common foods [5]. Also, Long-term consumption of melamine can result in kidney stone formation followed by Kidney failure, and possibly death could result from this ultimately. It can also result in cytomorphological changes, increased skeletal variance, cell growth inhibition, and decreased fetal weight [6].

Conventional food analysis methods offer efficient chemical trace analysis in food products with strong sensitivity, high reproducibility, and selectivity. These include Spectroscopy, immunoassay, colony counting, culture, enzymatic, nuclear magnetic resonance, electrochemical, and chromatography. However, the above said conventional methods require specialized equipment, time-consuming sample preparation, and skilled personnel [7]. Over a decade, nanomaterials may provide a way to design more precise, fast, and selective sensors in food analysis. Recently, Researchers have been attracted to optically sensitive nanomaterials, especially in food analysis, because of their unique properties like quick detection, easy operation, low cost, high specificity, and selectivity [8]. Due to the high sensitivity, simple equipment setup, easy operation, and identification of photoluminescence materials, the fluorescent method has proven to be a well-organized optical method for detecting analytes compared to other conventional analysis techniques [4].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently become an appealing ingredient for creating electrical, photonic, and biomolecular devices [9]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are defined as carbon-based zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials made up of one or a few nm thick graphene sheets with remarkable electrical and optical properties, mainly constituted of sp2-hybridized carbon. Furthermore, depending on the synthesis procedure, size, layered structure, chemical functionalization of their surfaces, and crystallinity, GQDs have a wide range of luminescence spectra with tunable fluorescence properties [10,11]. Because of their excellent properties, such as strong chemical inertness, fluorescence activity, luminescence emission, excellent biocompatibility, high surface area, and improved surface grafting, GQDs have been used in multidimensional applications such as designing sensors, bioimaging, photovoltaics, and energy storage devices. Although GQDs have numerous optical properties, they hold the properties of Quantum dots as they can also undergo photobleaching (if GQDs are exposed to light over time), and their photostability is unpredictable.

To overcome the above-said drawback and to improve the fluorescing properties of GQDs over a while, recently, researchers focussing much on luminescent nanomaterials doped GQDs as fluorescence sensing agents to provide excellent efficiency, high sensitivity, specificity, and simplicity without complicated operation.

On the other hand, Rare earth (RE) lanthanide compounds are promising materials with distinct mechanical, chemical, optical, and magnetic characteristics due to the gradual occupancy of 4f orbitals [12]. Because of their high sensitivity, photostability, and compatibility, RE lanthanide-doped materials have been used in sensing, imaging, multimodular imaging, LEDs, and drug delivery applications [1–11,13]. For instance, Huynh. et al. investigated Er-doped GQDs using the hydrothermal method for sensitive detection of Ferric ions in water and human serum [6]. Wen J et al. reported Cs-doped GQDs using the microwave method for specific detection of biological metabolites [14]. Llorent-Martinez et al. proposed an acidic-based chemical treatment method to synthesize novel terbium-sensitized highly luminescing GQDs to improve analyte sensitivity. Out of many rare earth lanthanide materials, Terbium and Europium were much more attractive due to their wide range of luminescence from ultraviolet (UV)- Visible to near-infrared spectral ranges (NIR) with fluorescence emission in the green and red regions of the electromagnetic spectrum. However, the above-said materials have numerous advantages; the terbium-doped nanomaterials were rarely investigated for sensing applications, especially in chemical sensing in food products [15].

To bridge this gap, we attempted to synthesize rare-earth terbium-doped Graphene quantum Dots (Tb-GQDs) as a fluorescence agent for ultrasensitive detection of toxins present in food products. We are prepared using domestic microwave and simple chemical conjugation methods. Citric acid and Diethylenetriamine (DETA) have been used as precursor materials. The as-synthesized Tb doped GQDs were characterized using UV–Vis spectrophotometer, Fluorescence emission spectroscopy, X-ray Diffractometer (XRD), High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) with EDS, Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) and Raman spectroscopy. Further, the rare earth terbium-doped graphene quantum dots were used as a fluorescence-based sensing probe for the sensitive detection of raw melamine and its presence in different milk samples.

The citric acid (CA,99 %) was purchased from SDFCL Mumbai, Diethylenetriamine (DETA, 98 %) was purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories Mumbai, Terbium Chloride (TbCl3·6H2O,99.9 %) was obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific USA, Glycerol (99 %), Melamine (99 %), and Sodium citrate (99 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide (97 %) was purchased from SDFCL, and Trichloroacetic acid (%) was obtained from Sisco Research Laboratories. Double Deionized water (DI water) was used throughout the experiment.

The physicochemical properties of GQD and Tb-doped GQDs were studied using various analytical methods viz., X-ray diffractometer, HR-TEM (High-Resolution Transmission Electron microscopy), UV–visible- NIR spectrophotometer, fluorescence steady state and lifetime measurements, respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were done using Bruker D8 advance instrument with a 2.2KW Cu anode radiation source (l = 1.541A8) and a Lynx-eye position-sensitive detector. High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) (JEM 2100F, JEOL) is used to study the nanomaterials' size, shape, and inner structure. UV–Visible- NIR spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu) and Fluorescence Emission spectroscopy (Hitachi F-7000, slit width- 5 nm) are used to observe absorption and emission spectra of doped/ undoped GQDs. Raman Spectroscopy was performed (Horiba Scientific France, XploRATM plus with an incident laser source at a 532 nm green laser and scanned the wavelength regions from 1000 to 2000 cm−1 at RT. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopic analysis was carried out using an FT-IR-6600 type A instrument from Jasco Inc., India. The fluorescence lifetime measurements were done using a time-correlated single photon counting system (Fluorolog- 3, Horiba Jobin Yvon IBH, UK) with a fast response red sensitive Photo Multiplier Tube (PMT, Hamamatsu Photonics, Japan) detector for an excitation wavelength of 290 nm for GQDs and 300 m for Tb-GQDs, respectively. The fluorescence decay was observed at 443 nm and 545 nm.

A simple and cost-effective Graphene quantum dot was synthesized using the microwave method, as reported previously by Wen et al. [16]. Briefly, 0.25 M of Citric acid (CA), 0.46 of Diethylenetriamine (DETA), and 1.4 M of Glycerol were dissolved in 10 mL DI water. The mixture is kept under constant stirring until the mixture turns homogenous. The homogenous solution was held in the microwave oven for 5 min to initiate carbonization. During this process, the color of the solution changes from colorless to yellow and then turned into brownish-black in color. The final product was washed twice with ethanol and deionized water and filtered using a 0.22 µm microporous membrane (Scheme.1 ).

Tb- GQDs were synthesized using a modified procedure proposed by Mondal et al. [17]. In brief, 2 mg/ml of GQDs were diluted with 10 mL of DI water and were kept under a magnetic stirring process at RT. Subsequently, 5 mg of Terbium chloride hexahydrate was added to the above-said solution until it became homogenous. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 7 upon gradually adding NaOH. Meanwhile, 2 mg of sodium citrate is added to the solution under stirring to avoid coagulation, and continue the stirring process at 80◦C for another 3 hrs. The resultant solution was cooled to room temperature.

Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M Sulfuric acid was chosen as the standard for the quantum yield calculation using the given formula:
ϕ s
=
ϕ r
(
I s
I r
)
(
A r
A S
)
(
η S
η r
)
2
where 'I' is the measured Integrated emission intensity, 'ɸ' is the quantum yield, 'η' is the refractive index of the solvent (η = 1.33 for water), and 'A' is the optical density. The subscript “s” and “r” are sample and reference standards. The quantum yield of the reference Standard-Quinine sulfate is 54 %.

Initially, a stock concentration of Melamine and Tb-GQD nanocomposites was fixed as 6.3 µg/mL (50 µM) and 50 µM/ml. Different melamine ranges from 0 to 5 µM from the stock was diluted in distilled water and titrated to a fixed concentration of 400 µL (from 50 µM/ml) of Tb- GQDs. After mixing, the fluorescence emission spectra of the solution are taken at an excitation wavelength of 300 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of melamine in different preserved milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the concentration of melamine in the milk samples, the cow milk and the labelled milk samples are pre-treated based on the previous report by Dai et al. [4]. In brief, the centrifuge tubes were added with 1 mL of 2 M trichloroacetic acid and 5 mL of different milk samples. The samples were sonicated and centrifuged for 10 min to precipitate the protein residues and separate the supernatant. The pH of the obtained solutions is then adjusted to 7 using NaOH. The final solution was filtered through a 0.22 µMfilter to get a clean supernatant.

The XRD spectra of both GQD and Tb-GQD are shown in Fig. 1 (i). From the XRD figure, it is clearly demonstrated that there is a presence of a broad diffraction peak (002) around 25° (2 theta) with an inter-lattice spacing of 0.41 nm. This peak may indicate a formation of graphite structure due to the citric acid carbonization [18]. The diffraction peak broadens due to the amorphous nature of the carbon structure [19]. In addition, the sharp rise with increased intensity around 32 degrees confirms the dopant presence in the XRD pattern of Tb-GQDs and that of GQDs.

The HRTEM images GQD and Tb- GQD are shown in Fig. 0.1 (ii). Lattice fringes are observed for both materials with a lattice spacing of 0.276 nm and 0.2 nm, respectively [20–22]. These values correspond to the (100) plane of the graphene structure and the obtained results suggest the presence of graphitic sp2 carbon atom crystal cores [23]. The synthesized GQDs and Tb-doped GQDs are spherical with sizes of 2 nm and 7 nm, indicating increased GQDs due to the terbium moieties. In addition to this, the EDS spectrum and the elemental analysis were analysed and shown in Fig. S4.

The FTIR spectra were observed to confirm the conformational changes in the structure of GQD due to the doping of terbium onto it. The FTIR spectra of GQDs and Tb-GQDs are shown in Fig. 0.1 (iii). The peak bands at 3231 cm−1 and 2980 cm−1 indicate the formation of an O-H bond due to the presence of water medium and C-H stretching vibrations of GQDs. Further, the resultant spectra show a peak at 1641 cm−1 sharpened due to C = C stretching and π–π ∗ transition in GQDs. The 1641 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1 bands indicate C = C stretching and C-H bending [20,21,34]. Peaks seen in the ranges 1000–1400 cm−1 suggest the existence of the C-O, C- OH functional groups, respectively. On the other hand, the FTIR spectrum of Tb- GQD shows a similar spectral nature as GQD with minor peak shifts. The transmittance (%) of GQD decreases in the presence of terbium moieties, confirming the structural changes in GQDs due to the dopant material.

UV–visible absorption spectra are recorded to study the optical and photosensitive properties of the materials. Fig. 2 . (i) shows the absorption spectra of GQD, terbium-doped GQD, and melamine. In Fig. 2. i(a) blue line indicates that the Graphene quantum dot exhibits two clear peaks with strong absorption at 228 nm and 380 nm. These peaks can be formed due to the π–π∗ (K band) transition of the aromatic sp2 domains in C = C bonds and the n-π∗ (R band) transition of C = O bonds found in the sp3 hybrid region in the graphene structure [24–28]. Both GQDs and Tb-GQDs are well dispersed in water and show maximum intensity at blue and green electromagnetic spectral regions when a UV light hits the sample (Fig. 2. i (a,b) inset). In the case of Tb- GQD, the absorption maximum was observed at λ = 356 nm, which has evident peak broadening and blue shift (hypsochromic) in the maximum absorption region than that of pure GQD (Fig. 2. i (a)). The absorbance of Tb- GQD has doubled compared to that of GQD. The absorption spectrum of melamine is shown in Fig. 2. i(c), indicates that the compound has weak/ no absorbance. On the other hand, the absorption spectra of Tb-GQDs in the absence and presence of melamine with a red shift (bathochromic) at λmax = 356 nm, confirming the interaction between the nanocomposites and the melamine (see supporting information S1). This shift may be due to the Vander Waal force of attraction between Tb-GQDs and Melamine [29].

FT-IR and Raman are two supporting techniques progressively used to understand the functional and structural groups on various carbon nanostructures. Fig. 2. ii (a–c) shows the Raman spectra of the synthesized GQD and Tb- GQD in the absence and presence of melamine, respectively. The Raman spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 2. ii (a)) indicates the D and G bands around 1373 cm−1 and 1652 cm−1 related to sp3 and sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms. The D band may arise due to the dangling bond vibrations between carbon–carbon atoms and disorders/ defects found on the surface of GQD. Further, the GQDs formed a G band due to the phonon mode of sp2 carbon atom vibrations. The band at 2525 cm−1 denotes the second-order D (2D) band presence in GQD due to two-phonon lattice vibration [20,21,24,35].

On the other hand, in GQD structure modified with terbium functionalization (Fig. 2. ii(b)), there is an increase in intensity at the D band and G bands are associated with the rise in GQDs size, with that of nude GQDs. Also, the modified GQDs show a bathochromic (red) shift towards a longer wavelength (3 nm) at the D band with no shift at the G band. Further, the Raman spectrum of melamine-doped Tb – GQDs was analyzed to know the conformational/vibrational changes in both the D and G bands of Tb-GQDs due to the presence of melamine in Fig. 2. ii (c). We found no shift change at the D band and a hypsochromic & hypochromic shift at the G band, respectively. The peak shift in the Raman spectra indicates a significant interaction between the surface modifier (terbium moieties) on GQDs and also due to melamine molecules with Tb-GQDs, respectively. The ID/IG ratio increases from 0.83 to 0.85 for the prepared GQDs vs. Tb-GQDs with and without Melamine.

The excitation and emission spectra of both GQDs and Tb-GQDs are represented in Fig. S2(i, ii). Fig. 2 (iii, iv) shows the fluorescence spectra of GQDs and Tb-GQDs excited at different wavelengths ranging from 300 to 425 nm. Upon excitation with different wavelengths, the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs show excitation-dependent tunable emission properties with maximum fluorescence intensity at 457 nm was observed when excited at 375 nm. This increase in fluorescence intensity at 457 nm may be due to the tight confinement of the пelectrons in the sp2 clusters of GQDs [30–32]. In contrast, the fluorescence spectra of Tb-GQDs were recorded upon excited at a different wavelength; while exciting the Tb-GQDs at 300 nm, a dual peak exists around 455 nm (GQD region) and a small peak at 545 nm (Terbium region). The emission at 545 nm associated with the electronic transition of 5D3 to 7Fj (j = 5, 4, 3) denotes the presence of Terbium ions in the synthesized Tb-GQDs. On the other hand, the Tb-GQDs gives doubled emission intensity around 452 nm than that of GQDs when excited at 320 nm; this may be due to the carboxylic group reduction, which aids in non-radiative recombination centers of electron- holes pair [33], [39] Hence the excitation wavelength of λexc = 300 nm was used throughout the experiment. The fluorescent Quantum yield of Tb- GQD is calculated as 52 % of quinine sulfate as a reference.

As the above optical studies predict the considerable variation in Tb-GQDs in the absence and presence of melamine, their interaction mechanism was studied to evaluate the binding/quenching nature using UV–visible absorption and Fluorescence Emission Spectroscopy. The UV–visible absorption spectra of Tb-GQD with varying melamine concentrations (0–5 µM) are shown in Fig. 4 (i). The absorption maximum of Tb-GQDs and Melamine is observed around 356 nm and 240 nm, are found in Fig. 4 (inset). The absorption peak of Tb-GQD in the absence and presence of varying melamine concentrations (viz., 0––5 μM) shows an increase in absorbance at 356 nm with a blue shift towards the melamine region. This shift may be due to the intermolecular π–π interaction between the Tb-GQD and Melamine. In addition, there is a slight increase in absorbance around 300 nm, which denotes the presence of terbium moieties, and it also undergoes binding with the melamine molecules through an interaction mechanism.

Further, the nature of the quenching mechanism was analyzed using fluorescence emission spectroscopy. Fluorescence emission spectra of Tb-GQD with varying melamine concentrations are shown in Fig. 4(ii). The Tb-GQDs are excited at a wavelength of 300 nm, which gives a dual emission spectrum of around 443 nm (GQD emission region) and 545 nm (Terbium fluorescing region), respectively (S3 (i, ii)). Fig. 4(ii) shows that the fluorescence emission intensity maximum of Tb-GQDs (50 μg/ ml; λex = 300 nm excitation) gradually decreases and blue-shifted towards a shorter wavelength as the melamine concentration increases from 0 to 5 μM. This decrease in fluorescence due to the increase in the concentration of melamine may be due to the interaction between the presence of amine groups in Tb-GQDs and triazine groups of Melamine. It shows that even a minute concentration of melamine gives a distinct quenching in maximum fluorescence emission intensity. The slight blue shift in the peak from 443 nm to 439 nm confirms the presence of melamine attached to Tb-GQD.

The Stern-Volmer (S-V) equation was used to analyze the magnitude and nature of fluorescence quenching of Tb-GQD in the presence of melamine using the fluorescence emission spectra by the following formula:
(i)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
s v
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
(ii)
Log
(
F 0
- F )
F
= L o g
K
a
+ n
L o g
[ Q ]

Where [Q] is the quencher (Melamine) concentration, F0 and F are the fluorescence maximum intensity of Tb-GQD in the absence and presence of Melamine, Kq, Ksv, and τ0 are the quenching rate constant, Stern- Volmer constant, and the average lifetime of Tb-GQDs in the absence of quencher, respectively. Fig. 4 (iv: inlet) displays the Stern – Volmer plot based on the fluorescence maximum of Tb- GQDs quenched by the different concentrations of melamine. The plot between F0/F and concentrations of melamine indicates an upward curve with positive deviation towards axis Y (using Eq- i). Hence, to determine the nature of quenching, the modified Stern- Volmer equation (Eq-ii) was undoubtedly used to get the linear plot [36,37]. Fig. 4. iv shows the plot between log [Q] versus log [ (
F 0
- F ) / F ] gave a straight line, and from the linear plot, the binding constants Ka = 1.09 × 101 and the number of binding sites n = 2.3631 are calculated.

The quenching rate constant was calculated using.
(iii)
K q
=
K
sv
τ 0
where Ksv value from the Stern- Volmer plot is 5.884 × 106 L mol−1, the average lifetime of Tb- GQDs was 9.34 ns, and using the formula, the Kq is found to be 6.3 × 1014 L/mol s−1 [36,37]. Generally, suppose the Kq value was higher than the upper diffusion control limit of 2.0 × 1010 L/mol s−1. In that case, it denotes that the fluorescence quenching of Tb-GQDs was purely based on the static quenching process and also determines the possibility of selective/ specific binding of melamine molecules in Tb-GQDs. This evidence Tb-GQDs may be an effective alternative probe for detecting adulterants in food samples.

The limit of detection (LOD) was derived using the linear plot between (F0-F)/F0 vs. melamine concentration (0–5 µM) which is shown in Fig. 4. (iii) and the formula to calculate LOD is as follows;
(iv)
LOD = 3 σ / m
where σ represents the standard deviation of the sample and m is the slope of a straight line. The slope is obtained from the plot F0-F/F0 vs. concentration of melamine. From the figure, it is observed that there is a linear relationship between them with a linear correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9986). The limit of detection is obtained as 0.31 µM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of melamine in real-time sample
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze whether the spectroscopic method could be employed for melamine detection, we evaluated the melamine content determination in actual milk samples using Tb- GQDs (10 μM). In general, no predictable amount of melamine was found in the normal milk sample, so the milk samples were spiked with various concentrations of melamine viz. 1 µM, 2 µM, 3 µM, 4 µM, 5 µM &10 µM respectively. Using Beer Lambert’s law, the melamine concentration in the spiked milk samples in the absence and presence of Tb-GQDS were analyzed after considering the peak maximum absorbance at 234 nm [38]. In this experiment, the recovery rate percentage ranges from 100.25 % to 115.4 %, depending on the sample type was obtained, indicating that the proposed method was reliable and accurate in melamine detection (Table 2 ).

We successfully synthesized Terbium (Tb) doped GQDs in this work using microwave and simple chemical conjugation methods. The resultant Tb-GQDs were characterized by various physico-chemical methods and used to detect melamine in real-time milk samples. Based on the results from XRD and HR-TEM, the size and shape of the GQDs and Tb-GQDs were confirmed and found to be approximately spherical with a particle size of 2–7 nm. From the UV–visible spectroscopic studies, the absorbance peak of Tb-GQD at 356 nm increases, broadening, and blue shifts towards melamine (λmax = 240 nm) and terbium (λmax = 300 nm) regions, indicating the interaction between Tb-GQDs and the adulterants. These peak shifts may be due to the intermolecular π–π interaction between the Tb-GQD and Melamine. Further, the fluorescence emission spectra show a fluorescence quenching upon an increase in the concentration of melamine with a significant LOD value and found that the nature of the quenching was static. The average lifetime of GQD, Tb-GQD, and Tb-GQD with Melamine are 2.9 ns, 9.34 ns, and 7.89 ns, respectively. The slight decrease in the lifetime of Tb-GQD in the presence of melamine compared to that of Tb-GQDs indicates the increase in the hydrophobic environment around the nanocomposites. Additionally, a practical analysis of melamine in milk was carried out by UV–Vis spectrophotometer, showing a reasonable recovery rate with higher reliability and accuracy. Thus, from this study, we inferred that the doping of terbium with Graphene Quantum dots (GQDs) was found to be an effective and ultra-sensitive fluorescence probe with the ability to detect significantly lower concentrations (in the ranges of μM – nM) of melamine, quick response/ recovery rate leads us to step forward to design compatible/ portable sensors that could detect adulterant in food and water.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) for providing a Start-up Research Grant to Dr. Mangaiyarkarasi Rajendiran, Centre for Nanotechnology Research, VIT- Vellore with project file number SRG/ 2021/ 002073.
The author(s) acknowledge VIT-Vellore for providing VIT SEED GRANT for this research work.
Dr. Shanmugavel Chinnathambi was supported by a Grant-in-Aid for Early- Career Scientists fund (KAKENHI No. 22K15249) by Japan Society for the Promotion of Science.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109670.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
